content:
Hi,

I recently started working on a project for which I want to insmod my rust modules into my own kernel. For this I want to compile the rust-for-linux kernel and boot it with grub. I followed the tutorial of Wedson Almeide Filho (chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https://project.linuxfoundation.org/hubfs/Webinars/Webinar_Slides/LF-Writing-Linux-Kernel-Modules-in-Rust.pdf?hsLang=en), except here a busybox is used. This explains ofcourse why this command is perfectly usable before entering menuconfig in his case:

make allnoconfig qemu-busybox-min.config rust.config

For my usecase I tried to use different approaches which sadly failed...

  1. When using hte original command my compiled kernel won't boot and grub will get stuck after loading the initial ramdisk
  2. When using "make LLVM=1 oldconfig rust.config" I can compile my kernel but no kernel modules are present when I boot. Strangly everything still works in my VM. I can also see that CONFIG_RUST=n since it depends on some other parameters which are not set correctly (MODVERSIONS should be n). When setting these manually I sadly did not get any results. I get the same result with olddefconfig ins tead of oldconfig. Manually disabeling MODVERSIONS in the .config file does not seem to work.

Now is my question: does anyone know a way how I can fix this problem so I can boot my rust-for-linux kernel and load my rust modules?

By "main kernel", do you mean enabling Rust in your host kernel, i.e. for your physical machine? Or in a VM?

When setting these manually I sadly did not get any results.

Typically, you want to enable them through menuconfig (you can search RUST with / and it will show you the dependencies currently being met or not, which is useful to debug your config).

If you just want to quickly boot a kernel in a VM for some basic testing I recommend using virtme-ng. It uses copy-on-write versions of your host file system mounts in the VM so you don't even need to build a rootfs or initrd. In particular, this means that any modules that you build are easily accessible for loading.

I want to enable rust in the kernel of my VM. Fortunatly I managed to solve it by using the .config of 6.3.0-generic and changing "MODVERSIONS" to n in my .config file and manually adding CONFIG_RUST=y, though I don't know if this is the right way to do it.

Valentin Obst said:

If you just want to quickly boot a kernel in a VM for some basic testing I recommend using virtme-ng. It uses copy-on-write versions of your host file system mounts in the VM so you don't even need to build a rootfs or initrd. In particular, this means that any modules that you build are easily accessible for loading.

Thanks for the tip, I will definitly look into this.
